name: Palm Beach County, Florida
tagline: Florida's third-most-populous county (~1.5M residents, West Palm Beach) runs central IT through Information Systems Services — now branded the Office of Technology and Innovation — operating the largest county fiber network in Florida amid a fresh administrator and a CIO leadership transition.
overview: Palm Beach County is the third-most-populous county in Florida — roughly 1.5–1.6 million residents across West Palm Beach and 39 municipalities — and one of the largest counties by land area east of the Mississippi. County government operates under a County Administrator (CEO model) reporting to an elected seven-member Board of County Commissioners, with a workforce of about 7,100. Central IT is delivered by Information Systems Services (ISS), now publicly branded the Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI), which serves most departments under the Board plus some constitutional officers. The shop runs the largest county-owned fiber-optic network in Florida, a unified-communications platform for roughly 10,000 users, an inventory of 350+ in-house applications, Oracle databases, enterprise GIS, data-center colocation and hosting, and a 24x7 help desk. Its stated highest priorities are cybersecurity, broadband/connectivity, and centralized IT-security oversight. For a Dell SLG seller, Palm Beach is a large, infrastructure-rich county account in transition — a new County Administrator (Joseph Abruzzo, mid-2025) and a CIO succession (longtime CIO Archie Satchell retired January 2026; deputy now acting) — where the buying motion runs through the county Purchasing Department and cooperative contracts rather than the state's central vehicle.

budget.intro: Palm Beach County adopted a $9.6B total budget for FY2026 (Oct 1, 2025–Sep 30, 2026). The General Fund operating portion is about $2.5B, balanced at a countywide rate of 4.5000 mills — held flat from FY2025 — generating roughly $1.5B in ad valorem taxes (about $112M more than the prior year). IT capital costs are borne directly by the Board of County Commissioners rather than allocated through the ISS cost plan, so technology spend is split between ISS's recovered operating costs and Board-funded capital.

stats.label: Total county budget (FY26)
stats.note: All funds; includes enterprise funds, grants, and transfers — not all discretionary

stats.label: Countywide millage (FY26)
stats.note: Held flat from FY2025; generates ~$1.5B in ad valorem revenue

notes: The county weighed cuts and new revenue options against the FY2026 budget; flat millage on rising values still expands the revenue base, but net-new IT spend competes against other priorities — co-op pricing and ROI framing matter.

Capital IT costs are fully borne by the Board of County Commissioners and are not part of the ISS cost-allocation plan — map Board-funded technology capital separately from the ISS operating/cost-recovery line.
A precise ISS/OTI department operating budget was not confirmed from a current public source; anchor IT-specific spend against the adopted budget book rather than a single quoted figure.

FY2027 outlook is under acute fiscal pressure: County Administrator Joseph Abruzzo presented a proposed FY2027 budget in June 2026 with roughly $35M in cuts after the Commission directed a flat-or-lower millage, and county leaders warn a November 2026 statewide ballot measure raising the homestead exemption (to $150K in 2027, higher thereafter) could force the largest department cuts in county history — a discretionary-budget squeeze that will tighten net-new IT capital and sharpen ROI/co-op-pricing framing. Figures were still moving as of June 2026 (max-millage and property-value estimates due July 2026; final adoption September 2026) — treat as evolving.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & centralized security oversight
initiatives.summary: Cybersecurity is a stated top priority. The Enterprise IT Security division operates on the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (Identify/Protect/Detect/Respond/Recover) and is working to improve centralized oversight of IT security across departments and constitutional officers — an opening for security hardening, monitoring, identity, and resilience.

initiatives.name: Fiber network & connectivity
initiatives.summary: ISS/OTI builds and maintains the largest county-owned fiber-optic network in Florida and treats broadband/connectivity as a top priority — a driver for network infrastructure, edge, and the underlying compute/storage that rides on it.

initiatives.name: Data center, hosting & colocation
initiatives.summary: The county is a growing data-center and hosting provider not just internally but to other government, education, health-care, and nonprofit agencies (colocation, hosting, network/internet service). That as-a-service posture is a natural fit for ISG infrastructure refresh and hybrid-cloud conversations.

initiatives.name: Application modernization & AI readiness
initiatives.summary: With 350+ in-house applications, Oracle databases, and enterprise GIS to sustain, the county faces ongoing modernization and a regional AI/cyber talent buildout (Palm Beach State College's new AI Cybersecurity Center, 2026). Governance-first AI-infrastructure conversations land against that backdrop.

procurement.intro: Palm Beach buys through its own Purchasing Department, not the state's central vehicle. Confirm two things first: Dell's standing on the cooperative contracts the county leans on, and registration in the county's Vendor Self Service (VSS) system so you can see and respond to solicitations.

vehicles.name: Palm Beach County Purchasing Department
vehicles.detail: The county's central procurement authority — issues Invitations for Bid (IFBs) and Requests for Proposal (RFPs) for purchases at or above the $150,000 mandatory bid/proposal threshold, under the county Purchasing Code.

vehicles.name: Vendor Self Service (VSS) / Business Opportunities portal
vehicles.detail: The county's e-procurement system. Vendors register, select commodity codes, and receive email notifications of matching IFBs/RFPs; the Business Opportunities site is the one-stop for current solicitations.

vehicles.name: Cooperative & FL state contracts
vehicles.detail: Like most FL counties, Palm Beach leverages cooperative vehicles, piggyback contracts, and Florida state term contracts to buy IT off pre-competed pricing without a standalone RFP — confirm Dell coverage on the ones the county uses.

powerStructure.name: Joseph Abruzzo
powerStructure.title: County Administrator (CEO)
powerStructure.note: Selected June 2025, started mid-2025; prior Clerk of the Circuit Court & Comptroller and a former state legislator. Oversees ~7,100 staff and the $9.6B budget — the top executive relationship above ISS/OTI.

powerStructure.name: Michael (Mike) Butler
powerStructure.title: Acting CIO / Deputy CIO (ISS / OTI)
powerStructure.note: Promoted from CTO to Deputy CIO in Nov 2025 and acting CIO since Dec 2025 following Archie Satchell's retirement (Jan 16, 2026); 30+ years of county IT experience. The primary IT relationship while the permanent CIO role settles.

powerStructure.name: Tina Travieso
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (Enterprise IT Security)
powerStructure.note: Owns the NIST-CSF-based security program and the push for centralized IT-security oversight — the buying center for cybersecurity, identity, and monitoring.

powerStructure.name: Office of Financial Management & Budget (OFMB)
powerStructure.title: County budget
powerStructure.note: Owns the FY budget process and capital plan, including Board-funded IT capital — the gatekeeper for funding any net-new technology initiative.

powerStructure.name: Board of County Commissioners
powerStructure.title: 7-member elected board
powerStructure.note: Approves the budget, millage, and large contracts. Major awards ultimately need Commission approval.

news.title: Abruzzo pitches FY2027 budget with ~$35M in cuts; county braces for historic reductions if property-tax amendment passes
news.impact: Following a Commission directive to hold or lower the countywide millage, the County Administrator floated ~$35M in FY2027 cuts in June 2026 and warned that a November 2026 statewide ballot measure raising the homestead exemption could trigger the largest department cuts in county history (leaders cited a potential drop from ~$609M to ~$210M across 30 departments over two years). Expect a tighter discretionary/IT-capital environment — lead with ROI, consolidation, and cooperative pricing; figures were still moving (max-millage/value estimates due July, adoption in September).

news.title: County Administrator Abruzzo publicly disputes state CFO Ingoglia's 'wasteful spending' claims
news.impact: Abruzzo challenged the state CFO's shifting waste estimates ($344M then $444M) and offered a line-by-line budget review — signaling heavy state-vs-county fiscal scrutiny that will keep every large county expenditure, including IT, under a value-and-justification lens.

news.title: Longtime CIO Archie Satchell retires; Deputy CIO Mike Butler steps in as acting CIO
news.impact: A 35-year county veteran exits the CIO seat (retired Jan 16, 2026) with his deputy acting in the role — re-map the IT relationship and watch for the permanent CIO decision before forecasting large bets. The county's OTI page still lists Butler as Deputy CIO with no permanent CIO named as of late June 2026.

news.title: County adopts $9.6B FY2026 budget at a flat 4.5000-mill countywide rate
news.impact: Flat millage on rising values expands the revenue base, but the county is weighing cuts and new revenue — ROI and co-op pricing framing matter for net-new IT spend.

news.title: Palm Beach State College launches $1M federally funded AI Cybersecurity Center
news.impact: Regional AI/cyber capacity and a first-of-its-kind county cyber range signal rising local demand and talent for AI-enabled security — useful context for security and AI-infrastructure conversations.

dellActions: Re-map the IT relationship through the CIO transition — engage acting CIO Mike Butler now and track the permanent-CIO decision before timing large infrastructure or platform bets.

Lead with the county's stated priorities — cybersecurity and centralized security oversight, fiber/connectivity, and data-center/hosting — tying Dell ISG infrastructure and security to the NIST-CSF program under CISO Tina Travieso.

Position the county's data-center, colocation, and hosting-as-a-service posture as an ISG and hybrid-cloud refresh opportunity, including the capacity it resells to other government/education/health/nonprofit agencies.
Confirm Dell's standing on the cooperative and Florida state contracts Palm Beach uses, and register in the county's VSS / Business Opportunities portal so you can see and respond to IFBs/RFPs early.

Engage the new County Administrator (Joseph Abruzzo) and OFMB on the FY2026 budget posture — frame net-new IT against Board-funded capital and co-op pricing given the county's cost-and-revenue scrutiny.
